ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03311737); no specific Thai milk tea subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific Thai milk tea code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for Thai milk tea exists in the clinical terminology. No code assigned.

preparation_method: mixed with condensed milk and evaporated milk
main_ingredients: Thai tea leaves (Ceylon tea with spices and food colouring)

serving.natural_language_portion: One jumbo serving of Thai milk tea is approximately 600 ml (600 g), reflecting the oversized cup format sold at Thai beverage stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Iced Tea with Milk (Cha Yen) — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central reference entries for brewed black tea, sweetened condensed milk, and evaporated milk, combined with published recipe proportions for Thai milk tea
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai milk tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: brewed black tea (USDA FDC #171917), sweetened condensed milk (USDA FDC #170859), and evaporated milk (USDA FDC #172217), blended in proportions consistent with standard Thai milk tea recipes (approximately 60% brewed tea, 20% condensed milk, 20% evaporated milk by volume, before ice dilution). Per-100g values reflect the mixed beverage prior to ice melt dilution. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened condensed milk-based beverages and analogous sweet milk tea — estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened condensed milk (GI ~61) and sweet milky tea (GI ~50–55)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Thai milk tea (cha yen) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous entries: sweetened condensed milk GI ~61 (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) and sweet milky tea GI ~50 (University of Sydney GI Database). The beverage is dominated by rapidly absorbed sugars from condensed milk and added sugar, supporting a low GI estimate, though the high carbohydrate load results in a very high GL. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 105.0) / 100 = 57.75, rounded to 57.8. Carbohydrate per 600 g serving = 17.5 × 6 = 105.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~228 mg per jumbo serving)
hypertension.guidance: Thai milk tea is low in sodium (~38 mg per 100 g; ~228 mg per 600 ml jumbo serving), so it does not pose a direct sodium-related hypertension risk, but the high sugar content may contribute to weight gain and metabolic risk factors that indirectly elevate blood pressure with regular consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar content (~16.8 g sugar per 100 g; ~101 g sugar per jumbo serving) and low glycemic index (~55) with very high glycemic load (~58)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Thai milk tea in jumbo size, as a single 600 ml serving delivers approximately 101 g of free sugars and a glycemic load of ~58, which will cause a significant postprandial blood glucose spike and is incompatible with glycaemic management goals.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content and moderate saturated fat from condensed and evaporated milk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of Thai milk tea in jumbo portions contributes substantially to daily free sugar intake (far exceeding the WHO recommended limit of 25 g/day per serving) and provides moderate saturated fat (~8.4 g per jumbo serving), both of which are associated with dyslipidaemia and increased cardiovascular risk when consumed habitually.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai milk tea (ชานมไทย, cha yen or cha nom Thai) is a popular Southeast Asian sweetened beverage originating from Thailand, made by brewing strong Thai tea — a blend of Ceylon black tea leaves with spices such as star anise, tamarind, and orange blossom water, often with added food colouring that gives the tea its characteristic deep orange hue — and then mixing it with sweetened condensed milk and evaporated milk, sweetened further with sugar, and served over crushed ice. The jumbo version, as sold at Thai restaurants and beverage stalls in Singapore (e.g., Saap Saap Thai), is approximately 600 ml and represents a significantly larger portion than a standard serving. The beverage has a rich, creamy, sweet flavour with a slightly spiced tea aroma and a smooth, velvety texture from the dairy components. Nutritionally, a jumbo serving is very high in free sugars (approximately 101 g), providing around 570 kcal, making it a high-calorie, high-sugar drink. It contains moderate amounts of calcium and potassium from the milk components. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low), but the glycemic load per jumbo serving is very high (~58) due to the large carbohydrate volume. Thai milk tea is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or those on calorie-restricted diets. It is vegetarian and typically halal. It is widely consumed as a refreshment drink, dessert beverage, or accompaniment to Thai meals.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai milk tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for brewed black tea, sweetened condensed milk, and evaporated milk blended in standard Thai milk tea proportions. GI is estimated from analogous University of Sydney GI Database entries. Confidence is medium due to estimation from components rather than direct measurement of the prepared beverage.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (brewed black tea FDC #171917, sweetened condensed milk FDC #170859, evaporated milk FDC #172217) blended in proportions consistent with standard Thai milk tea recipes. No direct laboratory analysis of this specific product is available. 2. The serving size of 600 g reflects the jumbo format sold at Saap Saap Thai - Funan on field survey data. 3. `dietary_fibre_g` is 0.0 as tea-based beverages with milk contain negligible dietary fibre; confirmed zero per USDA component data. 4. `trans_fat_g` is 0.0 as confirmed negligible in the dairy and tea components per USDA FoodData Central. 5. `vitamin_d_mcg` is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation. 6. `vitamin_e_mg` is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation. 7. `selenium_mcg` is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources used for this estimation. 8. GI is set to 55 (low) based on analogous University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened condensed milk and sweet milky tea; no direct GI measurement for Thai milk tea exists in the approved GI database. 9. Glycemic load per jumbo serving (600 g) = (55 × 105.0) / 100 = 57.75 ≈ 57.8, where 105.0 g carbohydrate = 17.5 g/100g × 600 g. 10. FoodOn match type is closest_parent (FOODON_03311737 milk tea); FoodEx2 match type is closest_parent (A0FKP tea-based beverages); SNOMED CT match type is none as no specific clinical concept exists. 11. The food is Thai cuisine; mandatory Thai-script local name is provided (ชานมไทย (ไซส์จัมโบ้)).
